content.title: ZERO ARTICLE (NO ARTICLE)
content.description: Used when we speak about things in a general sense or with specific categories.

content.subtitle: With the names of games, sports, and academic subjects or with some words and expressions: work, home, TV, to have breakfast/lunch/dinner.
content.description: Example: I go to work by bus.

content.title: DEFINITE ARTICLE: THE
content.description: Used with singular, plural, and uncountable nouns when the speaker and listener both know exactly which thing is being discussed.

content.subtitle: Second mention
content.description: Example: I saw a movie. The movie was long.

content.subtitle: Superlatives
content.description: Example: He is the best student.

content.title: INDEFINITE ARTICLE: A / AN
content.description: Used with singular countable nouns when mentioning something for the first time or talking about things in general.

content.subtitle: A - Before words starting with a consonant sound.
An - Before words starting with a vowel sound.

secondSection.title: Adjective
secondSection.description: An adjective is a word that describes or modifies a noun or pronoun, adding details about its qualities, characteristics, quantity, or extent.

content.description: go after the noun and complete a linking verb

thirdSection.title: Noun
thirdSection.description: A noun is a word that names a person, place, thing, or idea (concept, quality, state, action) and serves as a fundamental building block in language, often acting as the subject or object in a sentence.

content.title: PROPER
content.subtitle: (London, Monday)
content.description: Specific names for people, places or things.

content.title: COMMON
content.subtitle: (park, mom)
content.description: General names for people, places or things.

content.title: CONCRETE
content.subtitle: (tree, cat)
content.description: Things that you can see, touch, hear, smell or taste.

content.title: ABSTRACT
content.subtitle: (happiness, bravery)
content.description: Ideas, feelings or qualities you cannot perceive with your senses.

content.title: COLLECTIVE
content.subtitle: (family, team)
content.description: Groups or collectives of people or things.

fourthSection.title: Verb
fourthSection.description: A verb is a word that expresses an action, occurrence, or state of being, forming the core of a sentence and indicating what the subject is doing or experiencing.

content.title: STATIVE VERBS:
content.subtitle: describe a state or feeling
content.description: Example: He knows the correct answer. He still believes that story.

content.title: LINKING VERBS:
content.subtitle: connect the subject to more info

fifthSection.title: Tenses
fifthSection.description: Tenses are verb forms that show when an action or state occurs (past, present, future) and provide details about its duration or completion.

content.title: Future:
content.subtitle: Will happen later
content.description: e.g. will walk, will be eating

content.title: ASPECTS (DETAILS)
content.description: These combine with the main tenses to add nuance.

content.title: Perfect:
content.subtitle: Completed action, often with present relevance

content.title: Perfect Continuous:
content.subtitle: Ongoing action that started in the past

content.title: Past Continuous:
content.subtitle: S + was/were + V-ing
content.description: I was writing a book all day long.

content.title: Present Simple:
content.subtitle: S + V +(s/es)
content.description: I write books. She writes books.

content.title: Present Continuous:
content.subtitle: S + am/is/are + V-ing
content.description: I am writing a book now.

content.title: Present Perfect Continuous:
content.subtitle: S + have/has + been + V-ing (+ since/for)
content.description: I have been writing a book.

content.title: Future Continuous:
content.subtitle: S + will/shall be + V-ing
content.description: I will be writing a book.

content.title: Future Perfect:
content.subtitle: S + will have + V3
content.description: I will have written a book by the end of the week.

content.title: Future Perfect Continuous:
content.subtitle: S + will have been + V-ing
content.description: I will have been writing a book.

seventhSection.title: Adverb
seventhSection.description: An adverb is a word that modifies or describes a verb, adjective, another adverb, to provide more detail.

content.title: ADVERBS DESCRIBE VERBS
content.description: Example: Abdullah finished his test hastily.

content.title: ADVERBS DESCRIBE ADJECTIVES
content.description: Example: Mount Ranier is quite tall.

content.title: ADVERBS DESCRIBE ADVERBS
content.description: Example: The hotel is almost fully booked.

eighthSection.title: Adverbs are words that answer QUESTIONS like:

content.title: WHEN:
content.subtitle: last month, last week, now, later etc.

ninethSection.title: American and British English differences
ninethSection.description: American and British language differences relate to spelling, vocabulary, grammar, pronunciation, and cultural norms (directness versus restraint). British English often retains more traditional forms, while American English prefers simplification, although both are mutually intelligible.

content.title: R-Coloring (Rhoticity):
content.description: (Non-Rhotic): Vowels before 'r' often lengthen or change, but the 'r' isn't pronounced unless followed by another vowel (e.g., car /kɑː/).
(Rhotic): The 'r' sound is pronounced after the vowel (e.g., car /kɑːr/, bird /bɜːrd/).

content.title: "Short O" (ɒ) Sound:
content.description: Words like hot, lot, box use the /ɒ/ sound.
“O” becomes /ɑː/ (like father) or /ɔː/ (like bought) (e.g., hot /hɑːt/ or /hɔːt/).

content.title: Diphthongs (Vowel + 'r'):
content.description: Diphthongic transitions are preserved, for example: near /nɪər/.
The /ə/ sound often disappears to form a long vowel or monophthong, for example: near /nɪr/.

content.title: Verbs
content.description: use present perfect more ("I've just eaten").
use simple past tense ("I just ate").

content.title: Word endings
content.description: retains French roots: -our, -ise, -re, -ce (colour, organise, centre, defence).
simplified and differentiated spellings: -or, -ize, -er, -se (color, organize, center, defense).

content.title: Double consonants
content.description: often doubles consonant in verbs when adding endings (travelling, cancelled)
usually uses a single consonant (traveling, canceled).
